projects.title: ReBELS
projects.fullTitle: Resolving Biological carbon Export in the Labrador Sea
projects.description: Understanding how winter convection and spring restratification control biological carbon export in the Labrador Sea using autonomous platforms and ship-based observations.
projects.summary: ReBELS, a project funded by the UK Natural Environmental Research Council (NERC), is a New Investigator Standard Grant awarded to NOC (and BAS as partner). It aims to understand and quantify the transport of oceanic organic carbon into the deep sea and its effect on ocean carbon storage. Using a year-long ocean observatory in the Labrador Sea — combining autonomous observing technologies (gliders and biogeochemical Argo floats) with moored sediment traps — ReBELS will quantify the relative contribution of gravitational, eddy subduction and mixed layer particle injection pumps to overall carbon export, and identify the key processes that must be represented in climate models to predict North Atlantic carbon drawdown with confidence.

projects.title: POLOMINTS
projects.fullTitle: Polar Ocean Mixing by Internal Tsunamis
projects.description: Investigating the role of internal waves and mixing in polar ocean biogeochemistry and carbon cycling.
projects.summary: POLOMINTS investigates the role of internal waves — generated by tidal flow over rough seabed topography — as drivers of diapycnal mixing in polar oceans. By combining moored observations, autonomous platforms and numerical modelling across the Arctic and Southern Ocean, the project quantifies how this mixing shapes nutrient supply, primary production and the biological carbon pump in regions critical to global climate.

projects.title: PARTITRICS
projects.fullTitle: PARTIcle Transformation and Respiration Influence on ocean Carbon Storage
projects.description: Quantifying particle transformation processes and their impact on carbon export efficiency in the twilight zone.
projects.summary: PARTITRICS addresses a fundamental gap in our understanding of ocean carbon storage: what happens to sinking organic particles in the twilight zone (100–1000 m). By combining in situ optical sensors, sediment traps and incubation experiments across contrasting Atlantic regimes, the project quantifies how particle disaggregation, zooplankton repackaging and microbial respiration determine the depth at which carbon is remineralised and the fraction that reaches the deep ocean.

projects.title: IDAPro
projects.description: Integrating physical and biological drivers of productivity across Atlantic Ocean basin.
projects.summary: IDAPro develops an integrated understanding of how physical forcing — from basin-scale circulation to mesoscale eddies and fronts — controls phytoplankton productivity and carbon export across the Atlantic Ocean. Using a combination of sustained glider surveys, biogeochemical Argo floats and satellite data synthesis, the project produces observational constraints on productivity drivers that can be used to evaluate and improve Earth system models.

projects.title: GLIDERS I
projects.fullTitle: GLider-type Infrastructure and Digital Environment for Research Science
projects.description: Developing glider data pipelines for sustained ocean observation.
projects.summary: GLIDERS I establishes the data infrastructure and processing pipelines needed to make ocean glider observations FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). The project develops automated quality-control workflows, real-time data visualisation tools and standardised data formats, enabling glider data collected at NOC to feed directly into operational oceanography and research data archives.

projects.title: GLIDERS II
projects.fullTitle: GLider-type Infrastructure and Digital Environment for Research Science
projects.description: Expanding data pipeline capabilities to the Autosub Long-Range (ALR).
projects.summary: GLIDERS II extends the data infrastructure developed in GLIDERS I to the Autosub Long-Range (ALR) autonomous underwater vehicle, and scales the pipeline to support multi-platform, multi-mission deployments. The project also develops tools for integrated analysis of glider, ALR and Argo float data, supporting both real-time adaptive sampling and post-cruise data synthesis.

projects.title: APART
projects.fullTitle: Autonomous Platforms as A Research Tool
projects.description: Training and capacity building for autonomous platform deployment and data analysis.
projects.summary: APART provides structured training in the deployment and data analysis of autonomous ocean platforms — gliders, Argo floats and surface drifters — for early-career researchers across the UK. Through hands-on workshops, at-sea training and online resources, the project builds national capacity in autonomous observing technology and prepares the next generation of oceanographers for sustained ocean observation.

projects.title: TechOceans
projects.description: Developing and testing new sensor technologies for autonomous ocean platforms.
projects.summary: TechOceans was a European H2020 project developing and validating novel sensor technologies for integration on autonomous ocean platforms. The project brought together engineers, oceanographers and industry partners to advance miniaturised biogeochemical sensors — including optical nitrate, dissolved oxygen and particle sensors — and establish best practices for their deployment and calibration on gliders and Argo floats.

projects.title: SEAFAReRS
projects.fullTitle: Slocum Enhanced Adaptive Fe Algal REsearch in the Ross Sea
projects.description: Glider-based study of iron-limited phytoplankton dynamics in the Ross Sea, Antarctica.
projects.summary: SEAFAReRS used Slocum ocean gliders equipped with fluorescence and optical sensors to investigate iron limitation of phytoplankton growth in the Ross Sea. The project demonstrated the capability of adaptive glider sampling to resolve mesoscale features in phytoplankton biomass and physiology, and contributed to understanding of how iron supply controls primary production in Antarctic polynyas.

projects.title: BIARRITZ
projects.fullTitle: Bridging International Activity and Related Research Into the Twilight Zone
projects.description: International collaboration on twilight zone biogeochemistry and carbon export processes.
projects.summary: BIARRITZ was a NERC Global Partnerships Seedfund project that established collaborations between UK, US and international partners to advance understanding of twilight zone carbon cycling. The project facilitated joint data analysis, researcher exchanges and the development of shared observational strategies for quantifying mesopelagic carbon flux across ocean basins.

projects.title: GOCART
projects.fullTitle: Glider Observations and Connectivity Along a Reproductive Tract
projects.description: Using ocean gliders to study phytoplankton dynamics and productivity on the Agulhas Bank.
projects.summary: GOCART used ocean gliders to study the physical and biological controls on phytoplankton productivity along the Agulhas Bank, South Africa. The project combined glider-based fluorescence and optical backscatter measurements with ship-based sampling to characterise seasonal variability in phytoplankton biomass, community composition and carbon export in this highly productive eastern boundary system.

projects.title: COMICS
projects.fullTitle: Controls Over Ocean Mesopelagic Interior Carbon Storage
projects.description: Investigating mesopelagic carbon storage mechanisms in the South Atlantic and around South Georgia.
projects.summary: COMICS investigated the biological and physical controls on carbon storage in the mesopelagic zone (100–1000 m) of the South Atlantic. Using a combination of sediment traps, in situ pump casts, optical sensors and incubation experiments during two research cruises to South Georgia, the project quantified rates of particle export, remineralisation and zooplankton-mediated carbon flux, and assessed how these processes contribute to the ocean's biological carbon pump.

projects.title: CUSTARD
projects.fullTitle: Carbon Uptake and Seasonal Traits in Antarctic Remineralisation Depth
projects.description: Examining seasonal controls on carbon export and remineralization in the Southern Ocean.
projects.summary: CUSTARD examined how the seasonal cycle of stratification, mixing and biological production controls the depth at which organic carbon is remineralised in the Southern Ocean. Using sediment traps, optical sensors and incubation experiments deployed during a Southern Ocean cruise, the project resolved the temporal variability of carbon flux attenuation and identified the key biological and physical drivers of remineralisation depth in this carbon-critical region.

projects.title: PAL-LTER
projects.description: Long-term ecological research along the West Antarctic Peninsula examining climate impacts on marine ecosystems.
projects.summary: The Palmer Antarctica Long-Term Ecological Research (PAL-LTER) program investigates how year-to-year and decadal climate variability affects the marine ecosystem along the West Antarctic Peninsula — one of the fastest-warming regions on Earth. As a PhD student, fieldwork contributions included annual research cruises and an overwintering season at Palmer Station, studying phytoplankton photophysiology and primary production in relation to sea ice dynamics and mixed layer physics.

projects.title: NA-VICE
projects.fullTitle: North Atlantic Virus Infection of Coccolithophores Expedition
projects.description: Shipboard study of coccolithophore bloom dynamics and viral infection in the North Atlantic.
projects.summary: NA-VICE was a process study cruise in the North Atlantic investigating the dynamics of coccolithophore blooms and their termination by viral infection. Participation as a PhD student contributed phytoplankton photophysiology measurements (FIRe fluorometry) to characterise bloom health and light-use efficiency across bloom development and decline phases.

projects.title: CONVERGE
projects.fullTitle: Impacts of local oceanographic processes on Adélie penguin foraging ecology
projects.description: Linking local oceanographic variability to Adélie penguin foraging behaviour along the West Antarctic Peninsula.
projects.summary: CONVERGE investigated how mesoscale and submesoscale ocean features — fronts, eddies and convergence zones — influence prey aggregation and Adélie penguin foraging success along the West Antarctic Peninsula. As part of the PAL-LTER program, the project combined biologging data from instrumented penguins with concurrent physical and biogeochemical oceanographic surveys to link penguin behaviour to the underlying ocean environment.

projects.title: FIRe-Glider
projects.fullTitle: Fluorescence Induction and Relaxation on Gliders
projects.description: Developing and deploying fluorescence-based phytoplankton physiology sensors on autonomous gliders.
projects.summary: FIRe-Glider developed and validated a miniaturised Fluorescence Induction and Relaxation (FIRe) sensor for integration on ocean gliders, enabling continuous autonomous measurements of phytoplankton photophysiology. The project demonstrated that glider-mounted FIRe can resolve spatial and temporal variability in phytoplankton light-use efficiency and nutrient stress at scales previously inaccessible to ship-based surveys, opening new possibilities for large-scale monitoring of phytoplankton physiology.

themes.biophysical: Biophysical Controls on Marine Productivity and Carbon Export
